---
title: "Charge, voltage, current, and reference directions"
description: "Relate charge, electric field, potential difference, and current, with the reference directions that make every later circuit statement unambiguous."
chapter-id: F02
status: draft
---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity — draft"}
This chapter presents a complete instructional path: exposition, first-principles
derivations, worked examples, an executable check, a measurement plan with an
explicitly illustrative dataset, a fault diagnosis, and a full exercise set. It is
still subject to technical and editorial revision, and the physical evidence it
describes is collected in the aligned laboratory work, not in the prose. See how to
interpret status levels in the [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd).
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary for this chapter"}
Everything here stays within the **current-limited, extra-low-voltage** boundary
set in [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd): bench work under local supervision and
approved procedures. This chapter defines quantities and conventions; it is **not**
authorization to work on mains, high-voltage, high-energy batteries, RF power, or
any safety-critical system. Even at extra-low voltage, a low-impedance source or a
charged capacitor can release enough energy to heat conductors, damage equipment,
or cause burns. Establish both the voltage and available-energy boundary before
touching metal [@iec60479].
:::

## Central question

> When a circuit lights a lamp, charges a phone, or reads a sensor, what physical
> quantity is actually moving, what is merely changing, and how fast does each one
> happen?

Ask three careful people what "flows" in a wire and you will get three answers:
electrons, current, and energy. All three are involved, none of them is a synonym
for the others, and the differences between them are exactly what a beginner keeps
getting wrong — that a bulb "uses up current," that electrons "race at the speed of
light," that a wire is always at "one voltage." This chapter replaces those slogans
with four precisely defined quantities — **charge, electric field, potential
difference, and current** — and, just as importantly, with the **reference
directions** that let a signed measurement mean something. [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd)
promised that the sign of a reading is a claim about a direction you chose in
advance; this is the chapter that makes good on it.

::: {.callout-tip title="Make a prediction first"}
A lamp sits at the far end of a 3 m cord. You close the switch. Commit to three
answers before reading on. **(a)** When does the *electrical disturbance* reach the
lamp — nanoseconds, seconds, or minutes? **(b)** How fast does an individual
conduction electron drift along the wire while current is steady — a fraction of a
millimetre per second, a few metres per second, or near the speed of light? **(c)**
In steady operation, is the current leaving the lamp smaller than the current
entering it because the lamp "used some up"? Do not use the lamp's visible response
as the clock for part (a): an incandescent filament, an LED, and their drivers have
different response times after the electrical change arrives. Write the three
answers down before continuing [@horowitz2015art].
:::

## Learning outcomes

This chapter assumes only [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd) (the bench, SI units, and the
reference-direction habit) and the algebra and scientific notation of
[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd). After completing it you should
be able to:

- **define electric charge and state its conservation**, relate the coulomb to a
  count of elementary charges, and use both to do charge bookkeeping (builds on the
  SI base units of F01);
- **explain the electric field and potential difference** as *force per unit charge*
  and *energy per unit charge*, compute a voltage from a uniform field, and say why a
  single number — a node voltage — can describe a point at all (builds on the energy
  idea of F01);
- **define current as the rate of charge flow through a chosen reference direction**,
  read a signed current or voltage correctly, and apply the associated (passive) sign
  convention (this is the chapter F01's reference-direction habit was pointing to);
- **estimate carrier drift velocity and contrast it with signal-propagation speed**,
  and thereby say precisely what moves quickly and what moves slowly (a quantitative
  outcome, built on SI estimation);
- **explain how carrier density and mobility distinguish conductors,
  semiconductors, and insulators**, and use resistivity and $R=\rho L/A$ *as an
  estimate* to size a conductor (looking ahead to Ohm's law in
  [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd)); and
- **state the assumptions behind an ideal wire and a lumped circuit**, apply charge
  conservation at a node, and name the length-versus-wavelength condition under which
  the whole picture stops being valid.

## Relations among charge, electric field, voltage, and current

Charge is the conserved property at the centre of the story; the other quantities
describe its interaction or transport. @fig-quantity-chain lays out the vocabulary
on one page: the **field** is force *per unit charge*, **potential difference** is
energy transferred *per unit charge*, and **current** is the net charge crossing a
chosen surface *per unit time*. Keep those distinctions; the rest of the chapter
develops them.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-quantity-chain
%%| fig-cap: "Four related but non-interchangeable quantities. Charge Q is a conserved property; electric field E gives force per unit charge; potential difference V gives field work per unit charge after a polarity is declared; and current I gives the rate of net charge transport through a declared surface."
%%| fig-alt: "A vertical diagram with charge Q at the top as a conserved property. One branch leads to electric field E, force per unit charge, then to potential difference V, field work per unit charge. A separate branch leads from charge Q to current I, net charge crossing per unit time."
flowchart TB
  Q["Charge Q (C)<br/>conserved property"]
  E["Electric field E (V/m)<br/>force per unit charge"]
  V["Potential difference V (V)<br/>field work per unit charge"]
  I["Current I (A)<br/>net charge per unit time"]
  Q -->|"force on it: F = qE"| E
  E -->|"work to move it: W = qV"| V
  Q -->|"rate it flows: I = dQ/dt"| I
```

@tbl-quantities is the same map as a reference table; the derivations that justify
each "defining relation" column occupy the next three sections.

| Quantity | Symbol | SI unit | Defining relation | What it measures |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| electric charge | $Q,\ q$ | coulomb, C ($=$ A·s) | conserved property | net amount of positive minus negative charge |
| electric field | $\vec E,\ E$ | volt/metre, V/m ($\equiv$ N/C) | $\vec F = q\vec E$ | force a unit positive charge feels at a point |
| potential difference (voltage) | $V,\ v$ | volt, V ($\equiv$ J/C) | $V_{AB}=W_{A\to B}^{(E)}/q$ | field work per unit charge, with $+$ at A and $-$ at B |
| current | $I,\ i$ | ampere, A ($\equiv$ C/s) | $i = \mathrm{d}q_{\text{crossed}}/\mathrm{d}t$ | rate of net charge transport through a chosen surface |

: The four quantities this chapter defines, with their SI units and defining relations. The ampere is an SI base unit; charge is the derived quantity A·s. Vector symbols ($\vec E$, $\vec F$) carry a direction in space; circuit voltage and current carry signs relative to declared references. {#tbl-quantities}

## Electric charge and conservation

**Charge is a property of matter, like mass, but it comes in two signs and it is
conserved.** Two facts make it the bedrock of the whole subject.

*Ordinary circuit charge transfer is quantized.* Electrons and ions carry charge
in integer multiples of the elementary charge $e$, whose value the 2019 SI
redefinition fixes *exactly* [@bipm2019si]:
$$
e = 1.602176634\times10^{-19}~\text{C}.
$$
A proton carries $+e$ and an electron $-e$. Net charge transferred through an
ordinary wire is therefore counted in steps of $e$. (Fractionally charged quarks
are not isolated circuit carriers; some advanced solid-state systems have
fractional *quasiparticle* excitations, outside this chapter's scope.) The coulomb
is a very large count: one coulomb corresponds to
$$
\frac{1~\text{C}}{e} = \frac{1}{1.602176634\times10^{-19}}
   \approx 6.24\times10^{18}\ \text{elementary charges}.
$$
Because the count is astronomically large in ordinary macroscopic circuits, a
continuous-charge approximation is excellent there. Granularity becomes observable
when very small numbers of carriers or shot noise matter, as in photodiodes and
small devices (a thread picked up in
[A06](../02-analog/a06-noise-nonlinearity-stability.qmd)).

*Net electric charge is conserved.* Charged particles can be created, destroyed,
or transformed in physical reactions, but the algebraic total charge does not
change. At circuit scale we ordinarily move and separate existing charge: if net
charge leaves a defined region, the region's stored charge falls by exactly that
amount. This principle produces the continuity equation and, after a lumped-circuit
approximation is stated, Kirchhoff's current law. It is an exact conservation law;
the later approximation is that a circuit node stores negligible net charge
[@griffiths2017electrodynamics].

For stationary point charges in vacuum, the magnitude of their force is described
by **Coulomb's law** [@griffiths2017electrodynamics]:
$$
|F| = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\,\frac{|q_1 q_2|}{r^{2}},
\qquad \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\approx 8.99\times10^{9}~\text{N·m}^2/\text{C}^2,
$$ {#eq-coulomb}
where $r$ is their separation and $\varepsilon_0\approx8.854\times10^{-12}$ F/m is
the vacuum permittivity. The force lies along the line joining the charges: like
signs repel, unlike signs attract, and the magnitude falls as $1/r^2$. We will
rarely compute @eq-coulomb directly — circuits are built from *fields* and
*potentials*, which package Coulomb's law into more useful quantities — but it is the
reason those quantities exist.

::: {.callout-note title="Demonstration — reading Coulomb's law"}
Two point charges of $+1.0$ nC separated by 0.10 m in vacuum repel with
$$
|F|=(8.99\times10^9)
\frac{(1.0\times10^{-9})^2}{(0.10)^2}
=8.99\times10^{-7}~\text{N}\approx0.90~\mu\text{N}.
$$
Doubling the separation makes the force one quarter as large; reversing one
charge's sign leaves the magnitude unchanged but changes repulsion to attraction.
The calculation applies to point charges in vacuum. It is not a wire-force formula:
extended conductors rearrange surface charge, and material polarization changes the
field.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Worked example — charge bookkeeping in a battery cell"}
*Estimate.* Consider an illustrative cell with a rated capacity of 3.00 A·h. An
ampere-hour is current times time, hence charge, so a full rated discharge transfers
order $10^{4}$ C through the external circuit, or order $10^{23}$ elementary-charge
equivalents. State that before computing.

*Calculate.* Charge delivered:
$$
Q = 3.0~\text{A·h}\times 3600~\tfrac{\text{s}}{\text{h}} = 1.08\times10^{4}~\text{C}.
$$
Number of elementary-charge equivalents: $Q/e =
1.08\times10^{4}/1.602\times10^{-19}=6.74\times10^{22}$. In a metallic external
wire, electrons carry that charge; ions also move inside the electrochemical cell.

*Check.* Units: A·h·(s/h) = A·s = C. ✓ Order of magnitude: $10^4$ C and $10^{23}$
charge equivalents, as predicted. At a 1.00 A draw the cell lasts
$Q/I=1.08\times10^4$ s
$\approx 3$ h, consistent with "3 A·h at 1 A ≈ 3 h." ✓

*Interpret.* The carrier count is unwieldy, which is why circuit work uses coulombs
and amperes. The 3.00 A·h value is **illustrative**, not a specification for an
unnamed cell. A real datasheet defines test current, cutoff voltage, temperature,
aging conditions, and whether the stated capacity is typical or guaranteed; the
simple 3 h estimate is valid only if the rated capacity applies at the stated 1.0 A
operating condition.
:::

## Electric field and potential difference

A bare charge tells you nothing about what it will *do*; that depends on the other
charges around it. Rather than re-run Coulomb's law over every pair, we summarize
"the effect of all the other charges at this point" in one vector, the **electric
field** $\vec E$, defined using a positive test charge small enough not to perturb
the original charge distribution:
$$
\vec E = \frac{\vec F}{q}, \qquad [\,E\,] = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{C}}
   = \frac{\text{V}}{\text{m}}.
$$ {#eq-field}
A positive charge placed in the field feels $\vec F = q\vec E$ along the field; a
negative charge feels a force *against* it. The two unit spellings N/C and V/m are
identical, and the second one already hints that the field is a voltage spread over
distance.

**Voltage is energy per charge, but its sign requires a declared polarity.** Define
$V_{AB}$ with $+$ at A and $-$ at B, so $V_{AB}\equiv V_A-V_B$. If the electric
field moves a positive test charge $q$ from A to B, the work done *by the field* is
$W_{A\to B}^{(E)}=qV_{AB}$. Equivalently, the charge's potential-energy change is
$\Delta U_{A\to B}=-qV_{AB}$. Therefore
$$
V_{AB} = \frac{W_{A\to B}^{(E)}}{q}
=-\frac{\Delta U_{A\to B}}{q},
\qquad [\,V\,] = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{C}} = \text{volt}.
$$ {#eq-voltage}
One volt is one joule per coulomb. When one coulomb moves through a potential drop
of one volt, the field transfers one joule. The *magnitude* of the energy change for
one electron crossing a one-volt difference is one **electron-volt**,
$1~\text{eV}=e\times1~\text{V}=1.602176634\times10^{-19}$ J exactly. Whether the
electron gains or loses potential energy depends on its direction and negative
charge.

For a **uniform electrostatic field**, choose A and B a distance $d$ apart with the
field pointing from A toward B. The force on a positive test charge is constant, so
the field work from A to B is $W_{A\to B}^{(E)}=qEd$. With $+$ at A and $-$ at B,
@eq-voltage becomes
$$
V_{AB} = E\,d \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad E = \frac{V_{AB}}{d}.
$$ {#eq-ved}
This is why the field's unit is volts per metre: 100 V across an idealized 1 mm
uniform-field gap corresponds to $10^5$ V/m. Do not turn that ratio alone into an
insulation claim: breakdown also depends on geometry, pressure, humidity, material,
surface condition, and transient duration.

::: {.callout-note title="Demonstration — polarity controls the energy sign"}
Let A be 9.0 V above B, so the declared voltage is $V_{AB}=+9.0$ V. If the field
moves $+2.0$ C from A to B,
$$
W_{A\to B}^{(E)}=qV_{AB}=(2.0~\text{C})(9.0~\text{J/C})=18~\text{J},
$$
and the charge's potential energy changes by $-18$ J. Reverse the reference:
$V_{BA}=-9.0$ V. Nothing physical changed; the sign changed because the named
polarity changed. A negative test charge reverses the force and energy signs again.
This is why a voltage number without its two points and polarity is incomplete.
:::

Two properties of voltage matter for the rest of the book.

**Only differences are physical; a reference is a choice.** @eq-voltage defines a
*difference* between two points. The "voltage at a node" is shorthand for its
difference from a **reference node** we agree to call 0 V (the "ground" or "common" of
F01). Choosing a different reference shifts every node voltage by the same constant
but changes no voltage difference, current, field, or energy difference. A
differential measurement between the same two points is unchanged — exactly as
changing the zero of a height scale changes no separation. Declare the reference
before you quote a node voltage.

**In electrostatics the field is conservative, so potential is path-independent.**
The field work per charge between A and B is the same along every path
[@griffiths2017electrodynamics]. That is what permits one scalar potential at each
point. When magnetic flux through a loop changes with time, however, Faraday's law
gives nonzero electric-field circulation around the loop. A measured terminal
voltage then depends on the physical measurement path as well as its endpoints.
Lumped circuit theory remains useful when the loop is small enough that this induced
term is negligible; otherwise a ground symbol hides important geometry. That is the
reason F01 treated the measurement loop as part of the circuit, and it returns in
[F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd).

## Electric current and its reference direction

**Conduction current** is the signed rate at which net charge crosses a chosen
surface, such as the cross-section of a wire. Choose the positive normal to that
surface first, and let $q_{\text{crossed}}(t)$ be the algebraic charge that has
crossed in that direction. Then
$$
i(t) = \frac{\mathrm{d}q_{\text{crossed}}}{\mathrm{d}t},
\qquad\text{and over an interval}\qquad I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t},
\qquad [\,I\,] = \frac{\text{C}}{\text{s}} = \text{ampere}.
$$ {#eq-current}
One ampere is one coulomb per second — about $6.24\times10^{18}$ elementary charges
of one sign crossing per second in the simple single-carrier picture. The ampere is
the electrical SI base unit, and since
2019 it is defined by fixing $e$ (@eq-coulomb's charge) together with the second
[@bipm2019si]. For steady DC, instantaneous and average current coincide. The
derivative form (calculus reviewed in
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd)) becomes essential the
moment a capacitor charges in [F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd), where
$i = C\,\mathrm{d}v/\mathrm{d}t$. No electron crosses a capacitor's dielectric;
changing electric flux supplies the corresponding displacement-current term in
Maxwell's equations. At capacitor terminals, circuit theory packages that effect as
the branch current $i$.

::: {.callout-note title="Demonstration — current accumulates charge"}
A steady $+20$ mA through the declared positive direction for 250 ms transfers
$$
\Delta Q=I\Delta t=(20\times10^{-3}~\text{C/s})(250\times10^{-3}~\text{s})
=5.0\times10^{-3}~\text{C}.
$$
That is $5.0$ mC, or $\Delta Q/e\approx3.1\times10^{16}$ elementary-charge
equivalents. If the same physical current flows opposite the reference arrow,
$I=-20$ mA and the signed $\Delta Q$ is $-5.0$ mC. The magnitude did not become
"negative"; the sign records direction.
:::

### Positive current reference direction

Current has a direction, and a number for it is meaningless until you say which way
counts as positive. **The reference direction is an arrow you draw on the branch
before you measure anything.** A measured value of $+15$ mA then means "positive
charge crosses in the arrow's direction at 15 mA"; a value of $-15$ mA means "the
actual flow is 15 mA *against* the arrow." Neither is wrong; the sign is information
about direction relative to *your* choice, exactly as F01 insisted for a voltmeter's
leads. @fig-reference-directions fixes such a choice for a one-loop circuit: the
current reference is clockwise, and the voltage across $R$ is marked $+$ where the
current enters.

![A single source–load loop with its reference directions declared *before* any measurement. The current reference $I$ is clockwise; the load voltage $V_R$ is marked $+$ at the terminal the reference current enters. With the current arrow entering the $+$ terminal, the *passive sign convention* applies to $R$, so a positive $V_R I$ is power the resistor *absorbs*. TP\_A and TP\_B are the test points where the two node voltages are measured against the 0 V reference.](../../../build/circuits/f02-reference-directions.png){#fig-reference-directions fig-alt="A voltage source VS with its plus terminal at the top drives a clockwise reference current I through a load resistor R and back to the 0 V reference node. The voltage across R is marked positive at the top where the current enters. Two test points, TP_A at the top node and TP_B at the 0 V node, are brought out to the right."}

The choice in @fig-reference-directions is not arbitrary in one respect: for a
*load* we conventionally point the current reference into the $+$ voltage terminal.
This is the **passive sign convention** (also called *associated* reference
directions), and it makes the product $p = vi$ come out **positive when the element
absorbs energy** and negative when it delivers energy. An ideal positive resistor
always absorbs, so
with associated references its power is positive; a source usually delivers, so its
power comes out negative under the same convention. We only *name* the convention
here — power itself is the subject of [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd) — but fixing
it now is what keeps the sign of every future energy statement honest. (These
quantities are DC or instantaneous values; when signals become sinusoidal in
[F08](f08-sinusoids-impedance-frequency.qmd), you must additionally say whether an
amplitude is peak, peak-to-peak, or RMS, a distinction F01 flagged and F08 formalizes.)
For a quick numerical reading, $v=+5.0$ V and $i=+20$ mA under associated
references gives $p=vi=+0.10$ W: the element absorbs energy at 0.10 J/s. If the
measured current were $-20$ mA with the same voltage reference, $p=-0.10$ W and the
element would deliver energy at that instant.

### Conventional current and electron drift

By a historical convention older than the electron, **current points in the direction
positive charge would move.** In a metal wire the mobile charges are electrons, which
are negative, so the electrons physically drift *opposite* to the conventional current
arrow. This is not a mistake to be corrected and not two competing currents: it is one
physical flow with two equivalent descriptions. A conventional current of $+1$ A to
the right *is* a stream of electrons moving left, because moving negative charge one
way is electrically identical to moving positive charge the other way. Every equation
in this book uses conventional current; you only need the electron picture when the
carrier's actual identity matters, as in a semiconductor junction
([A01](../02-analog/a01-semiconductor-diodes.qmd)), where electrons and positively
charged "holes" genuinely move in opposite directions and both contribute.

## Carrier drift and signal propagation

Here the opening prediction gets settled, and the answer is more interesting than
either slogan. Relate the macroscopic current to the microscopic motion of carriers.
Choose a positive axis along a uniform wire of cross-sectional area $A$. In a
single-carrier conductor with $n$ mobile carriers per unit volume, carrier charge
$q_c$, and signed mean drift velocity $v_d$ along that axis, the carriers crossing a
plane in time $\Delta t$ occupy a signed slab length $v_d\Delta t$. Multiplying
number density, volume, and charge gives
$$
I = n\,q_c\,A\,v_d, \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
J \equiv \frac{I}{A} = n q_c v_d,
$$ {#eq-drift}
where $J$ is the signed component of **current density** in A/m². For electrons,
$q_c=-e$ and their drift velocity is opposite conventional current, so the two
negative signs give positive $I$. When only the speed is wanted, use magnitudes:
$|I|=neA|v_d|$. This result is charge counting; the material physics enters through
the values of $n$ and $v_d$.

::: {.callout-note title="Worked example — slow drift and fast propagation"}
*Estimate.* Copper has about one free electron per atom, $n\approx 8.5\times10^{28}$
per m³ [@kittel2005solidstate]. A 1 A current in a thin hookup wire (AWG 24,
$A = 0.205~\text{mm}^2 = 2.05\times10^{-7}~\text{m}^2$) spreads that one amp over a
huge number of carriers, so each must move *slowly*. Guess sub-mm/s.

*Calculate.* Solve @eq-drift for the drift velocity:
$$
|v_d| = \frac{|I|}{neA}
= \frac{1~\text{A}}{(8.5\times10^{28})(1.602\times10^{-19})(2.05\times10^{-7})}
\approx 3.6\times10^{-4}~\text{m/s} = 0.36~\text{mm/s}.
$$

*Check.* Units: $\text{A}/(\text{m}^{-3}\cdot\text{C}\cdot\text{m}^2)
= \text{A}/(\text{C·m}^{-1}) = \text{A·m}/(\text{A·s}) = \text{m/s}$. ✓ At 0.36 mm/s a
given electron takes $1~\text{m}/(3.6\times10^{-4}~\text{m/s})\approx 2800$ s, about
**47 minutes**, to travel one metre.

*Interpret.* Closing the switch launches an electromagnetic disturbance through
the field in and around the two-conductor path. Using an **illustrative** propagation
speed of $2.0\times10^8$ m/s, the earliest electrical change reaches a lamp 3.0 m
away after about $3.0/(2.0\times10^8)=15$ ns [@ulaby2015electromagnetics]. That is a
propagation-delay estimate, not a claim that visible light appears in 15 ns: the
lamp and any driver add electrical, recombination, or thermal response time. As the
field changes locally along the cord, local electrons acquire their slow drift.
During 15 ns an electron drifting at the calculated steady speed would cover only
$(3.6\times10^{-4})(15\times10^{-9})\approx5\times10^{-12}$ m, a few picometres.
Thus the electrical influence propagates quickly while charge carriers drift
slowly. Energy transfer is described by the electromagnetic field; later chapters
formalize its rate as power.
:::

For the third prediction, scope matters. In a lumped series loop at steady state,
current is equal at every cross-section because the charge stored in each region is
no longer changing. During switching, tiny surface-charge changes and parasitic
capacitances help establish the new fields, so currents at separated cross-sections
need not match at the same instant. A lamp does not consume current: in steady
operation, charge enters and leaves at equal rates while electrical energy is
converted to light and heat. **Energy is transferred; net charge is conserved.**
We examine the steady claim with an illustrative dataset below.

## Charge transport in conductors, semiconductors, and insulators

Carrier density is important, but it is not the whole classification. A carrier
must also respond to a field. In the low-field, approximately linear regime, define
the **mobility** $\mu$ by the magnitude relation $|v_d|=\mu|E|$. Combining it with
@eq-drift gives, for one carrier type,
$$
J=\sigma E, \qquad \sigma=n|q_c|\mu, \qquad \rho\equiv\frac{1}{\sigma},
$$ {#eq-conductivity}
with conductivity $\sigma$ in S/m and resistivity $\rho$ in Ω·m. Multiple carrier
types contribute additively to conductivity. This compact relation assumes an
isotropic, homogeneous material, sufficiently small field, and stable temperature;
mobility can depend on temperature, field, impurities, and crystal direction
[@kittel2005solidstate; @sedra2020microelectronic].

| Class | Carrier picture | Why it is useful | What defeats the label |
|---|---|---|---|
| conductor | many mobile carriers with useful mobility | small field can support substantial current; used for wires, contacts, and planes | finite resistivity, heating, high frequency, and geometry |
| semiconductor | electron and hole populations can be changed by doping, fields, light, and temperature | controllable conductivity enables diodes, transistors, and sensors | operation is strongly condition- and device-dependent |
| insulator or dielectric | very few mobile carriers under rated conditions; polarization can still occur | separates conductors and stores electric-field energy | leakage, contamination, humidity, surface conduction, and breakdown |

: Conductors, semiconductors, and insulators are operating descriptions, not perfectly sharp species. Carrier density and mobility jointly set low-field conductivity; environment and applied field can change the classification in practice. {#tbl-materials}

Copper illustrates the conducting end: near room temperature it has roughly one
conduction electron per atom, $n\approx8.5\times10^{28}$ m⁻³, and a representative
resistivity near $1.7\times10^{-8}$ Ω·m. Intrinsic silicon at 300 K has electron and
hole concentrations of only order $10^{16}$ m⁻³, but doping and electric fields
make carrier populations controllable over wide ranges [@kittel2005solidstate;
@sedra2020microelectronic]. An insulator is not literally carrier-free: leakage and
polarization still occur, and its useful limits must come from the actual material
or component specification. Contamination and moisture can make a nominally
insulating surface conduct enough to corrupt a high-impedance circuit.

::: {.callout-note title="Demonstration — the small field inside a copper wire"}
For the 1.0 A, AWG 24 example, $A=2.05\times10^{-7}$ m², so
$$
J=\frac{I}{A}\approx4.88\times10^6~\text{A/m}^2.
$$
Using the representative room-temperature copper resistivity above,
$$
E=\rho J\approx(1.68\times10^{-8})(4.88\times10^6)
=8.2\times10^{-2}~\text{V/m}.
$$
A uniform 1.0 m length therefore drops about $EL=82$ mV, whereas a 1 cm length
drops about 0.82 mV. The limiting case is visible: as length or resistivity tends
to zero, so does the drop. This anticipates $R=\rho L/A$ in F03 and also shows why
"wire = one node" can be excellent over a centimetre yet poor over a long,
high-current return.
:::

**Inside a real conductor carrying current there is a field, and it exposes the
ideal-wire approximation.** In electrostatic equilibrium — no current — the
mobile charges rearrange until the field inside a conductor is zero; any leftover field
would keep pushing them [@griffiths2017electrodynamics]. When the conductor *carries*
steady current in the linear regime, an axial field remains to sustain the drift.
The better the conductor, the smaller that field for a given current density. In
the limit $\rho\to0$ it vanishes: an **ideal wire** has zero field along it, hence
zero voltage across it, hence its two ends are at *the same potential*. That
idealization is what lets us draw a wire as a single node — and knowing it is an
idealization is what lets us predict when it fails.

## Nodes, ideal wires, and the reach of a lumped description

A **node** is the set of points joined by ideal (zero-resistance) wire; by the
argument just made they share one potential, so the whole node has a single voltage.
A **branch** is a component between two nodes; a **loop** is a closed path. These three
words, and the two conservation laws attached to them, are the grammar of every
schematic and the subject of [F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd). One of the two laws is
already in hand.

**Charge conservation permits accumulation and accounts for it exactly.** Enclose a
physical junction in a small boundary and define branch currents as positive into
it. Conservation says
$$
\sum I_{\text{in}}=\frac{\mathrm dQ_{\text{stored}}}{\mathrm dt}.
$$ {#eq-continuity-node}
This is the integral continuity equation: any instantaneous imbalance changes the
charge stored in the enclosed region. In the **lumped ideal-node approximation**, the
region's charge storage is negligible or is represented explicitly by a capacitor
branch. Then $\mathrm dQ_{\text{stored}}/\mathrm dt\approx0$, giving Kirchhoff's
current law (KCL), $\sum I_{\text{in}}=0$ for algebraically signed currents.

@fig-node-currents declares $I_1$ into node $a$ and $I_2,I_3$ out, so
$$
I_1-I_2-I_3=\frac{\mathrm dQ_a}{\mathrm dt}
\quad\xrightarrow[\text{ideal node}]{}\quad
I_1=I_2+I_3.
$$ {#eq-kcl}
The steady series-loop statement — equal current at successive cross-sections — is
the two-branch case after accumulation and unrepresented leakage are excluded. F04
develops KCL as a network tool.

::: {.callout-note title="Demonstration — what an unbalanced node equation predicts"}
Suppose the declared currents in @fig-node-currents are $I_1=12$ mA into the node,
$I_2=5$ mA out, and $I_3=6$ mA out. Then
$$
\frac{\mathrm dQ_a}{\mathrm dt}=I_1-I_2-I_3=1~\text{mA}.
$$
If that imbalance lasted 2 ms, the enclosed region would gain
$\Delta Q=(1~\text{mA})(2~\text{ms})=2~\mu\text{C}$. A tiny physical junction
cannot generally accept that charge without its voltage changing, so the currents
or fields must respond. If $I_3$ instead becomes 7 mA, the derivative is zero and
the node can remain in steady state. KCL is therefore not a rule that deletes
accumulation; it is the zero-accumulation operating case of the conservation law.
:::

![Charge conservation at a junction. With the declared arrows, $I_1-I_2-I_3$ equals the rate of charge accumulation in the enclosed node region; the ideal-node approximation sets that rate to zero and gives $I_1=I_2+I_3$. The explicit dot marks a genuine three-conductor junction.](../../../build/circuits/f02-node-currents.png){#fig-node-currents fig-alt="Three resistor branches meet at a connection dot labelled node a. Current I1 points into the node; I2 and I3 point out. Their signed imbalance equals the rate of charge accumulation, and becomes zero for an ideal lumped node."}

The single-potential node and the "same current everywhere in series" both rest on a
hidden assumption: that the circuit is **lumped** — small enough that a signal crosses
it in a time short compared with how fast the signal itself changes, so that "the
voltage here" and "the voltage there" are the same *now*. Concretely, the lumped,
ideal-wire description holds while the physical size $L$ of the relevant current
loop is much smaller than the wavelength $\lambda = v/f$ of the highest significant
frequency $f$
(propagation speed $v\approx c$ on loose wire, slower in a dielectric)
[@ulaby2015electromagnetics]; the common engineering line is
$$
L \ll \lambda, \qquad
L < \frac{\lambda}{10}\ \text{as an initial screening rule}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad f < \frac{v}{10L}.
$$ {#eq-lumped}
The one-tenth-wavelength line is a heuristic, not an accuracy guarantee: acceptable
electrical length depends on allowed phase error, interconnect impedance, source and
load mismatch, and edge spectrum [@ulaby2015electromagnetics]. A digital signal's
clock frequency alone is insufficient; its rise and fall times determine the
significant high-frequency content. @fig-lumped-validity turns the screening test
into the choice it represents.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-lumped-validity
%%| fig-cap: "Screening a connection for lumped treatment. Compare the relevant loop length L with the wavelength λ = v/f at the highest significant frequency. Small electrical length supports a lumped approximation; otherwise propagation, return-path geometry, and reflections require a distributed description. The one-tenth-wavelength rule is only a first screen, not a promised error bound."
%%| fig-alt: "A decision flowchart. Begin with loop size L and highest significant frequency f. Compare L with wavelength lambda. If L is much smaller, use a lumped approximation and then check parasitics. If not, include propagation delay and transmission-line effects."
flowchart TB
  A["Circuit size L,<br/>fastest frequency f"]
  B{"L ≪ λ ?<br/>(λ = v ∕ f)"}
  A --> B
  B -->|yes| C["Lumped approximation:<br/>then check parasitics"]
  B -->|no| D["Distributed description:<br/>delay and reflections"]
```

::: {.callout-note title="Worked example — when does a benchtop stop being one node?"}
*Estimate.* A breadboard assembly spans about $L = 0.30$ m of wire, effectively in
air, so $v\approx c$. Below some frequency it is safely lumped; guess "tens to
hundreds of MHz."

*Calculate.* The screening boundary from @eq-lumped is $f = v/(10L) =
$(3\times10^{8})/(10\times0.30)
= 1.0\times10^{8}~\text{Hz} = 100~\text{MHz}$. On an FR4 PCB the signal slows to
an illustrative $v\approx1.5\times10^{8}$ m/s, halving the boundary to about
50 MHz. Actual PCB velocity depends on stack-up, field distribution, and frequency.

*Check.* Sanity by wavelength: at 1 MHz, $\lambda = c/f = 300$ m $\gg 0.30$ m, so the
board is $10^{-3}$ of a wavelength — emphatically lumped. At 2.4 GHz (Wi-Fi),
$\lambda = 0.125$ m $\approx L$ — emphatically *not*. ✓

*Interpret.* At 1 MHz the propagation phase across 0.30 m is small, but that alone
does not make the wire ideal: finite resistance, inductance, capacitance, and return
geometry can still matter. Near and above the screening boundary, or with fast
logic edges containing appreciable energy there, the two ends cannot automatically
be treated as the same node. The required accuracy decides how early to abandon the
approximation. Distributed reasoning is developed in
[R03](../06-realization/r03-signal-power-integrity-emc.qmd)
and [S07](../05-domains/s07-communications-rf-antennas.qmd) — flagged here so you know
the boundary of the tool you are about to use everywhere.
:::

## Measurement procedure: steady-current continuity

A prediction becomes evidence only when it is tested. A clean test of the claim
beginners most doubt is to compare steady current at several break points around a
series loop. The physical test belongs to Lab **L02**. The values below form a
**synthetic teaching dataset**, chosen to be internally consistent with the stated
setup. They demonstrate analysis and record structure; they are not observations
and do not qualify the physical claim [@horowitz2015art].

Configuration (the reproducible record
[T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd) asks for):

- Illustrative source reading: 5.00 V DC; current limit set to 50 mA.
- Illustrative series loop: $R_1=100.3$ Ω and $R_2=219.4$ Ω as measured before
  assembly; nothing else intentionally connected.
- Hypothetical meter specification: DC mA range, resolution 0.01 mA, series
  **burden resistance** approximately 2 Ω at this operating point, and maximum
  indication error ±(0.8 % of reading + 3 counts). A real record must identify the
  instrument, range, calibration state, and actual datasheet.

The prediction has two layers. Ideally $I = V/(R_1+R_2) = 5.00/319.7 = 15.6$ mA. But
an ammeter is an **intrusion**, the dual of F01's voltmeter loading: to read current
you break the loop and insert the meter, adding its burden resistance in series, which
*lowers* the current slightly. With a 2 Ω burden the loop becomes 321.7 Ω and
$I = 5.00/321.7 = 15.5$ mA. That small deficit is a *predictable systematic bias*, not
noise.

| Break point (meter inserted) | Ideal $V/(R_1+R_2)$ | With 2 Ω burden | Illustrative indication | Comparison |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| before $R_1$ | 15.6 mA | 15.5 mA | 15.54 mA ± 0.16 mA | consistent |
| between $R_1$ and $R_2$ | 15.6 mA | 15.5 mA | 15.51 mA ± 0.16 mA | consistent |
| after $R_2$ (return leg) | 15.6 mA | 15.5 mA | 15.55 mA ± 0.16 mA | consistent |

: Synthetic series-current indications at three break points. The ±0.16 mA entries are conservative rounded limits from the hypothetical accuracy formula at about 15.5 mA; they are not $k=2$ expanded uncertainties. A complete uncertainty evaluation would also include source voltage, resistance, burden, temperature, and repeatability. {#tbl-current-continuity}

Two patterns are deliberately visible in @tbl-current-continuity. First, the three
intervals overlap, as expected for equal steady current at all break points. Second,
their central indications sit below the no-meter calculation and near the
burden-inclusive calculation. The table therefore illustrates how a result could be
consistent with charge continuity *and* meter intrusion. It does not prove either:
that evidential rung requires real data, repeated observations, and the uncertainty
work of F06. If real readings disagree beyond their evaluated uncertainty, preserve
the disagreement and investigate leakage, changing source voltage, contact faults,
range-dependent burden, or an incorrect circuit record.

A short, dependency-free script makes the quantitative predictions reproducible and
their parameters visible rather than buried in a screenshot:

```python
# f02_quantities.py — parameterized checks for the F02 worked examples.
# Standard library only; run with:  python3 f02_quantities.py

ELEMENTARY_CHARGE = 1.602176634e-19  # C, exact in the SI
N_CU = 8.5e28                         # illustrative, 1/m^3
RHO_CU = 1.68e-8                      # near 20 C, ohm*m
C_LIGHT = 2.998e8                     # vacuum, m/s

def charge_equivalents(coulombs):
    "Number of elementary-charge equivalents in a charge magnitude."
    return coulombs / ELEMENTARY_CHARGE

def drift_velocity(current_A, area_m2, n=N_CU):
    "Carrier-speed magnitude from I = n*q*A*v_d."
    return current_A / (n * ELEMENTARY_CHARGE * area_m2)

def wavelength_screen_frequency(length_m, v=C_LIGHT, ratio=10):
    "One-wavelength-ratio screening boundary, not an error guarantee."
    return v / (ratio * length_m)

def conductor_area_boundary(current_A, length_m, drop_V, rho=RHO_CU):
    "Area at equality for a conductor-only DC voltage-drop budget."
    r_max = drop_V / current_A       # allowed return resistance
    return rho * length_m / r_max    # from R = rho*L / A

if __name__ == "__main__":
    per_coulomb = charge_equivalents(1.0)
    cell_count = charge_equivalents(10800)
    print(f"1 C corresponds to {per_coulomb:.3e} charge equivalents")
    print(f"10800 C -> {cell_count:.3e} charge equivalents")
    vd = drift_velocity(1.0, 2.05e-7)  # 1 A in AWG-24 copper
    travel_minutes = 1 / vd / 60
    print(
        f"drift velocity {vd*1e3:.3f} mm/s; "
        f"1 m takes {travel_minutes:.1f} min"
    )
    delay_ns = 3 / 2e8 * 1e9
    print(f"3 m propagation at 2e8 m/s: {delay_ns:.1f} ns")
    f_screen = wavelength_screen_frequency(0.30)
    screen_mhz = f_screen / 1e6
    print(f"lambda/10 screen, 0.30 m in air: {screen_mhz:.0f} MHz")
    a = conductor_area_boundary(0.5, 0.5, 10e-3)
    area_mm2 = a * 1e6
    print(f"20 C conductor area > {area_mm2:.2f} mm^2 for <10 mV")
# Expected output:
#   1 C corresponds to 6.242e+18 charge equivalents
#   10800 C -> 6.741e+22 charge equivalents
#   drift velocity 0.358 mm/s; 1 m takes 46.5 min
#   3 m propagation at 2e8 m/s: 15.0 ns
#   lambda/10 screen, 0.30 m in air: 100 MHz
#   20 C conductor area > 0.42 mm^2 for <10 mV
```

Change a number — the wire gauge, the current, the length — and the model reports the
new consequence before the bench does. That is the loop this book runs on every claim.

## Misconceptions and limits of the lumped description

A working idea earns trust by naming what breaks it. Three slogans cause most of the
confusion this chapter targets, and one genuine fault shows how to tell an idealization
from a defect.

**"A load uses up current."** False in steady lumped operation. The continuity
equation requires equal rates of charge entry and exit when the load's stored charge
is not changing. @tbl-current-continuity shows what a test of that claim should look
like; Lab L02 supplies physical evidence. A load transfers or converts energy, not
net charge. During a transient, unequal terminal currents can temporarily change
stored charge or feed parasitic capacitance, so the unqualified slogan "current is
always the same" is also unsafe.

**"Electrons move at the speed of light."** The worked copper example gives a
steady drift speed of 0.36 mm/s, with $c/|v_d|\approx8\times10^{11}$. The
electromagnetic disturbance propagates through the structure at a substantial
fraction of $c$, set by its geometry and materials. Keeping propagation distinct
from carrier drift is essential to later timing and transmission-line reasoning.

**"Conventional current has the arrow backwards."** Neither description is more true;
they are the same physics (moving $-q$ left $\equiv$ moving $+q$ right). The only error
is *mixing* them mid-calculation. Pick conventional current, as this book does, and
stay in it.

The genuine failure is subtler and expensive, and it attacks the idealization at the
heart of the chapter — that a wire is one node.

::: {.callout-caution title="Worked diagnosis — one ground, or two?"}
A 3.3 V sensor board shares a single return wire with a 0.5 A actuator back to the
supply (the topology of @fig-shared-return). An ADC referenced to the supply ground
reads the sensor about 12 mV high, drifting with the actuator. Two hypotheses:

- **Sensor offset** — the sensor has a genuine 12 mV calibration error; the reading is
  a property of the sensor and is *independent* of the actuator current.
- **Shared-return ground shift** — the return wire is not an ideal node. Its resistance
  $R_\text{ret}$ carries the actuator current $I$, so the sensor's local ground $G_1$
  sits above the supply reference $G_0$ by $I R_\text{ret}$, and the "extra" 12 mV is
  that offset, which should *scale with $I$*.

These make different predictions, so a discriminating test exists. Estimate the
suspect offset: a thin return, $R_\text{ret}\approx 25$ mΩ, at $I = 0.5$ A gives
$I R_\text{ret} = 12.5$ mV — matching the symptom. Now vary the actuator current: drop
it to 0.1 A and the offset falls to $\approx 2.5$ mV; raise it and the offset climbs
in proportion. It tracks $I$ → the ground shift is confirmed and the sensor is
exonerated. (The clinching direct measurement: a voltmeter from $G_1$ to $G_0$ reads
the offset outright — the two "grounds" are simply not the same node.) This is the
method of [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd) in miniature: *isolate a cause
by finding evidence that separates it from its rivals.*
:::

![The failure of the single-node idealization. The return conductor has real resistance $R_\text{ret}$ and carries the full load current $I$, so the load-side ground $G_1$ is not at the reference potential $G_0$ but above it by $I R_\text{ret}$. Two points both labelled "ground" are then at different voltages — the equipotential-node assumption has quietly failed.](../../../build/circuits/f02-shared-return.png){#fig-shared-return fig-alt="A source VS drives current I through a load resistor R_L, which returns through a resistor R_ret representing the shared return conductor back to the reference ground G0. The load-side return node G1 is brought out to a labelled test point; the voltage from G1 to G0 equals I times R_ret rather than zero."}

## Worked design: return-conductor sizing and voltage-drop margin

Engineering is deciding under constraints and defending the choice. The decision this
chapter equips you to make is a small but ubiquitous one: **size a shared return
conductor so that the "single ground node" idealization holds to within a declared
budget** — turning the fault above from an accident into a specified margin.

*Requirements (measurable, declared in advance).*

- The return carries $I = 0.5$ A over a length $L = 0.5$ m.
- The DC ground offset $V_G = I R_\text{ret}$ must stay **below 10 mV** at a
  maximum conductor temperature of 60 °C.
- The combined resistance of connectors and terminations in the shared return is
  specified not to exceed 2 mΩ and must be verified separately.
- For this illustrative calculation, copper has
  $\rho_{20}\approx1.68\times10^{-8}$ Ω·m and temperature coefficient
  $\alpha\approx3.9\times10^{-3}$ K⁻¹ near room temperature; these are
  representative values, not procurement limits [@kittel2005solidstate].

*Calculate the requirement.* The total allowed return resistance is
$R_\text{ret}<V_G/I=0.010/0.5=20$ mΩ. Reserving 2 mΩ for connections leaves
18 mΩ for the conductor. Estimate the 60 °C resistivity with the first-order
temperature relation
$$
\rho_{60}\approx\rho_{20}[1+\alpha(60-20)]
\approx1.94\times10^{-8}~\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}.
$$
Using $R=\rho L/A$ as an estimate (developed in
[F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd)), the conductor must satisfy
$$
A > \frac{\rho_{60}L}{18~\text{m}\Omega}
=\frac{(1.94\times10^{-8})(0.5)}{0.018}
\approx5.4\times10^{-7}~\text{m}^2=0.54~\text{mm}^2.
$$
Nominal AWG 20 area (0.518 mm²) is now slightly too small; the next listed option,
AWG 18 (0.823 mm²), provides useful margin. The calculation assumes uniform
cross-section and uses nominal wire areas.

*Alternatives and trade-offs.*

| Option | Estimated $V_G$ at 0.5 A, 60 °C | Verdict | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| AWG 24 shared return (0.205 mm²) | ≈ 25 mV | fails | thinnest, lightest |
| AWG 22 shared return (0.326 mm²) | ≈ 16 mV | fails | light |
| AWG 20 shared return (0.518 mm²) | ≈ 10.4 mV | fails narrowly | medium |
| **AWG 18 shared return (0.823 mm²)** | **≈ 6.9 mV** | **passes with margin** | thicker, heavier |
| Separate return for the sensitive reference | removes this shared $IR$ term | strong alternative | more routing |

: Sizing the shared return against a strict 10 mV budget. Estimates use $V_G=I(\rho_{60}L/A+2~\text{m}\Omega)$, $L=0.5$ m, nominal areas, and representative copper properties. Equality at 10 mV would not pass because the requirement says "below." A separate return removes this shared-resistance contribution but still has its own resistance and inductance. {#tbl-conductor-choice}

*Decision.* Choose the **AWG 18 shared return** for this bounded DC requirement, or
route the sensitive reference separately when the topology permits. AWG 20 looked
adequate at 20 °C with ideal connections but fails after temperature and connection
allowances are included. That change is precisely why requirements are checked
against operating conditions rather than nominal room-temperature values.

*Acceptance test.* At 0.5 A after thermal steady state at the maximum specified
conductor temperature, a suitable differential measurement from load ground to
reference ground must remain below 10 mV, including its evaluated uncertainty. The
single result that would
**falsify** the design: an offset that *exceeds* the budget, or one that *grows with
load current past the limit*, proving the return is not the near-ideal node the design
assumed.

*Limit of the claim.* This is a **DC, thermal-steady-state** claim about resistive
$IR$ drop for the stated length, current, temperature, and connection allowance. It
does not establish ampacity, insulation, mechanical suitability, or transient
performance. A fast-changing current also sees return-path inductance, and
$L\,\mathrm{d}i/\mathrm{d}t$ can exceed $IR_\text{ret}$ on a switching edge.
Sizing against inductance and high-frequency return paths is the subject of
[R03](../06-realization/r03-signal-power-integrity-emc.qmd); stating this limit is the
difference between an engineering claim and an overreach.

## Conservation, reference directions, and approximation limits

Four quantities, defined once and for all, and a habit that makes their signs
trustworthy — that is the whole of this chapter:

- **charge** is a conserved property; ordinary circuit transfer occurs in elementary
  steps of $e$, though a continuous approximation is excellent for macroscopic
  circuits;
- the **field** is force per unit charge, while **voltage** is field work per unit
  charge after two points and a polarity have been declared;
- **conduction current** is the signed rate of net charge transport through a
  declared surface; a negative result reports motion opposite the reference, not a
  failed calculation;
- conservation first gives
  $\sum I_{\text{in}}=\mathrm dQ_{\text{stored}}/\mathrm dt$; KCL is the lumped,
  negligible-accumulation case, not a denial that charge can accumulate; and
- an **ideal wire** is equipotential, but a real connection also has resistance,
  inductance, capacitance, propagation delay, and a return path. Treating it as one
  node is a bounded engineering approximation.

Each of these hands directly to a later chapter, and F02 sits early in the book's
dependency graph for that reason:

- the **reference directions and passive sign convention** are used, unchanged, to
  read every source and load in [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd) and to sign
  every branch in the Kirchhoff analysis of [F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd);
- **charge conservation at a node** (@eq-kcl) becomes Kirchhoff's current law in
  [F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd);
- **energy per charge** (@eq-voltage) and the passive sign convention become the power
  budget of [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd);
- the microscopic field-and-drift picture ($\vec J = \sigma\vec E$) becomes the
  macroscopic Ohm's law and resistance of [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd), and
  the *controllable* carrier density of a semiconductor becomes the diode and
  transistor of [A01](../02-analog/a01-semiconductor-diodes.qmd)–[A02](../02-analog/a02-transistors.qmd);
- the **ideal-wire / distributed-interconnect screen** (@eq-lumped) marks where
  [R03](../06-realization/r03-signal-power-integrity-emc.qmd) and
  [S07](../05-domains/s07-communications-rf-antennas.qmd) take over.

Along the book's **measurement spine** — F01 → F02 → F03 → F06 (see the
[part introduction](index.qmd)) — this chapter is the step that turns the quantities
you agreed to reason in into quantities you can *measure with a sign*. You now have the
nouns and verbs of every circuit statement that follows.

## Exercises

Work these with the estimate → calculate → check → interpret loop, and state the
assumptions, reference directions, and units every time. Full solutions and
live-assessment variants are kept outside the public book; a few self-check answers are
given so you can calibrate.

**Quick check (multiple choice)**

One best answer each; the key follows. These surface the chapter's common
misconceptions before the longer problems.

1. In a lumped series loop at steady state, the current leaving the lamp compared
   with the current entering it is
    a. smaller, because the lamp uses some up;
    b. larger, because the lamp adds energy;
    c. equal, because charge is conserved;
    d. zero, because the lamp blocks it.
2. In a copper wire carrying a steady 1 A, an individual conduction electron travels at
   roughly
    a. the speed of light;
    b. a few hundred m/s;
    c. a fraction of a mm/s;
    d. it does not move at all.
3. One volt is defined as
    a. one coulomb per second;
    b. one joule per coulomb;
    c. one newton per coulomb;
    d. one ampere per ohm.
4. A branch current is measured as $-8$ mA relative to its reference arrow. This means
    a. the measurement is faulty;
    b. 8 mA of positive charge flows *opposite* to the arrow;
    c. the current is 8 mA below full scale;
    d. no current flows.
5. The best first screening condition for treating an interconnect as one lumped
   node is
    a. the wire is made of copper;
    b. the current is DC;
    c. the wire is much shorter than the signal wavelength, $L\ll\lambda$;
    d. the voltage is below 50 V.
6. Two points on a board are both labelled "ground" yet a meter reads 12 mV between
   them. The most likely cause is
    a. the meter is broken;
    b. charge is being destroyed at one point;
    c. a shared return conductor carries current, so $I R_\text{ret}$ separates them;
    d. one point is at a higher *frequency* than the other.

*[Key: 1-c, 2-c, 3-b, 4-b, 5-c, 6-c. Item 1 maps to charge conservation and
@tbl-current-continuity; 2 to the drift-velocity example; 3 to @eq-voltage; 4 to
reference directions; 5 to the wavelength screen @eq-lumped; 6 to the shared-return
diagnosis.]*

**Retrieval and reasoning**

1. State, in one sentence each, what charge, field, voltage, and current *are* (not
   their units). Then name which is conserved and which is a rate.
2. Explain why "the voltage at node $a$" requires a reference node to be meaningful,
   and what changes physically if you move the reference — current, energy, both, or
   neither.
3. A colleague says "electrons flow through the wire at the speed of light." Correct the
   statement precisely: what travels at nearly $c$, and what actually crawls?

**Estimation**

4. A phone charger delivers 2.0 A through its metallic cable for 90 minutes. How
   many coulombs and elementary-charge equivalents cross a cable section?
   *[Self-check: $1.08\times10^{4}$ C, $6.7\times10^{22}$ charge equivalents.]*
5. Repeat the drift-velocity calculation of @eq-drift for a 10 A current in a heavier
   AWG 12 copper wire ($A = 3.31~\text{mm}^2$). Is the drift faster or slower than the
   1 A / AWG 24 case, and by roughly what factor? *[Self-check: $\approx 0.22$ mm/s;
   comparable — the larger area nearly cancels the larger current.]*
6. Estimate the highest frequency at which a 5 cm IC-to-IC trace on FR4
   ($v\approx1.5\times10^8$ m/s) can still be treated as one node using $L<\lambda/10$.

**Derivation and units**

7. Starting from "count the carriers in a signed slab of length $v_d\,\Delta t$,"
   derive $I=nq_cAv_d$ (@eq-drift). Confirm the units and show explicitly why
   $q_c<0$ and $v_d<0$ produce positive conventional current for electrons drifting
   opposite the positive axis.
8. From the definitions $E=F/q$ and $V=W/q$ and the constant force in a uniform field,
   derive $V_{AB}=Ed$ (@eq-ved) for a field pointing from A to B with $+$ declared at
   A. State the assumptions that make the derivation exact and one geometry where
   $E$ is not uniform.

**Data interpretation**

9. In the synthetic dataset @tbl-current-continuity the three indications are
   15.54, 15.51, and 15.55 mA, each with a stated ±0.16 mA instrument-error limit.
   Explain what the overlapping intervals show, why that limit is not automatically
   a $k=2$ uncertainty, and why the central indications lie below the no-meter
   prediction. Then state what additional records real evidence would require.
10. You measure 12.5 mV between two ground points at $I=0.5$ A and 5.1 mV at $I=0.2$ A.
    Show that the offset is consistent with a shared-return resistance, estimate
    $R_\text{ret}$, and state what a *constant* (current-independent) offset would have
    implied instead.

**Debugging**

11. A 2 × 4.7 MΩ divider from 10 V is probed and the mid-node reads far from 5 V.
    A colleague blames a "used-up current." Explain, using charge conservation and F01's
    meter-loading result, why current consumption is the wrong diagnosis and meter
    loading is the right one, and design one measurement that distinguishes them.
12. A logic board works with slowly changing signals but glitches after faster edge
    rates introduce appreciable spectral content near 300 MHz. Using @eq-lumped,
    explain why the "single ground node" approximation can degrade. Identify both
    $IR$ and $L\,\mathrm{d}i/\mathrm{d}t$ contributions and state what additional
    measurements or geometry are needed before claiming which dominates.

**Open design**

13. Specify a return-conductor scheme (shared vs star, gauge, and length) for a 3.3 V
    board with a quiet ADC reference and a 1 A motor driver, such that the ADC's ground
    stays within 5 mV of the supply reference. State your budget, the acceptance test,
    and the limit of the claim (what you are *not* guaranteeing).
14. Propose an experiment that tests "a lamp does not consume current in steady
    operation." Specify what you would measure, where, with what uncertainty, and
    what single result would *falsify* the bounded claim.

## Connections

Chapter ID: `F02`. Its one prerequisite is [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd) (the bench,
SI units, and reference directions), with just-in-time support from
[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) (algebra, units, prefixes) and, for
the instantaneous $i=\mathrm{d}q/\mathrm{d}t$, [M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd).
Downstream course mappings are maintained in [`chapters.toml`](../chapters.toml); the
[reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) provides reader-facing routes and the practice spine.

This chapter opens the middle of the **measurement spine** (F01 → F02 → F03 → F06)
described in the [Part I introduction](index.qmd). It hands its reference directions
and passive sign convention to [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd) and
[F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd); its charge-conservation law becomes Kirchhoff's
current law in [F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd); its energy-per-charge definition
feeds the power budget of [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd); its microscopic
field-and-drift picture becomes Ohm's law in [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd)
and the semiconductor behavior of [A01](../02-analog/a01-semiconductor-diodes.qmd); and
its lumped-versus-distributed limit is the entry point to
[R03](../06-realization/r03-signal-power-integrity-emc.qmd) and
[S07](../05-domains/s07-communications-rf-antennas.qmd).

The aligned practical evidence is **Lab L02** (source, load, resistance, and meter
loading), which asks you to measure series current continuity and voltage with declared
references and quantified instrument intrusion; it integrates, with F01 and F03–F06,
into milestone **M1** (a qualified energy-and-light measurement) with reproducible
records ([T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd)). Lab and project IDs
denote *evidence goals*, not permission to perform an activity: the safety level,
apparatus, stop conditions, and local authorization must be defined before any of this
becomes deliverable, and reading this chapter authorizes nothing beyond the
current-limited, extra-low-voltage boundary stated at its head.

## References

Claims are cited at the point of use and prefer primary or canonical sources. The
exact elementary charge, the ampere's 2019 definition, and SI usage rest on the
defining brochure and the NIST guide [@bipm2019si; @nist811]; the first-principles
treatment of charge, Coulomb's law, the electric field, the conservative electrostatic
potential, and current density follows the standard electrodynamics reference
[@griffiths2017electrodynamics]; free-carrier densities and resistivities of real
materials are drawn from the standard solid-state reference [@kittel2005solidstate],
with intrinsic-semiconductor behavior from a canonical microelectronics text
[@sedra2020microelectronic]; the lumped-versus-distributed criterion and
signal-propagation speed follow an applied-electromagnetics reference
[@ulaby2015electromagnetics]; and the bench-first working method and the danger of
low-voltage energy follow the standard practical reference and the physiological-current
standard [@horowitz2015art; @iec60479]. The complete, growing
[bibliography](../references.qmd) collects these. None of them, and neither this book,
substitutes for a datasheet, a governing standard, or supervised laboratory training.
